generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write to thee with a quill that trembles in my hand, for the tempest that howls upon the cliffs doth shake my very soul. Five years hath passed since we last spoke, and in that time, the world hath turned, and I, a man of stone and salt, hath not changed. Yet now, the wind doth howl like a thousand voices, and the sea doth roar as if it would swallow the very sky.**

I doth fear the night shall claim me, and the storm shall be my grave. Yet, I doth not fear for thee, for thou art the only light in this dark world, and I doth write this with the hope that the ink may reach thee, and the wind may not snatch it from thy hands.**

I have no words to say of love, for love hath been a thing of the past, yet I doth write of duty, for I am the keeper of this lighthouse, and it is my duty to guard the sea, even if it doth demand my life.**

Thy father hath not been a man of many words, but of many watches and many storms. I doth keep the lamp alight, though the lamp doth flicker, and the sea doth rage. I doth not know if I shall survive the night, but I doth know that if I doth not, then the light shall go

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I trust this letter finds you well, though I dares to believe it does. These past five years, we have not exchanged a word, and I must confess that I regret the silence that has grown between us. I have been a solitary man, you know, and the sea has been my only companion. Yet, even in my solitude, I have not ceased to think of you.**

**Tonight, the heavens grow dark, and the winds howl like the wails of a ghost. A storm is brewing, one that promises to be the fiercest I have known in my years of service here. I do not know if I shall survive it, but I do know that I shall not face it alone. I have always been one to stand my ground, and I shall not falter now.**

**I have not written this letter to merely inform you of my plight, though that is a part of it. It is to beg you to return, to come back to this lonely shore, and to find solace in my arms. I know that you have your own life, your own duties, and I do not wish to bind you to me. But I do wish for you to know that I have not forgotten you, and that I have not ceased to love you, even in my absence.**

**I have been given a task here, to keep the light burning, and I have done so, though the light

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I trust this letter finds you well, though I fear it may come too late. Five years have passed since our last correspondence, and I wonder if you still remember the days when we were once a family. I have not forgotten, though I have grown accustomed to silence. The world outside this tower has changed, but the sea remains the same—eternal, indifferent, and unyielding.**

Tonight, a tempest is upon us, one that has not been seen in these parts for many a year. The waves have grown fierce, and the wind howls like a banshee. I have lit the beacon with a steady hand, though my bones protest against the cold, and my heart, though it is not as strong as it once was, still beats for you, for the child we once had.**

I fear for my life, but I fear more for the thought that you may have moved on, that you may have found another shore to anchor your soul. I have not written to you in years, and I have no more words to offer, save for this one: I am sorry. I am sorry for the years I have wasted, for the love I have withheld, for the silence that has grown between us. I am sorry, Eleanor, and I hope, though it is too late, that you may yet find peace in your heart.**

May the storm pass soon, and may the dawn come with a promise
